text.title: Django Celery Tasks Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed when applying django-celery. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix keyed to the methodology.

antipattern.symptom: After a refactor that moves `tasks.py`, queued jobs deserialise to NotRegistered and dead-letter without alert.
antipattern.root-cause: @shared_task without name argument; module path baked into queued payload.
antipattern.fix: Add explicit `name=` to every task. Run a one-off script to backfill DLQ jobs under the new name.

antipattern.symptom: Customer received the same receipt email three times; charge endpoint debited twice.
antipattern.root-cause: Task retried on transient broker disconnect; no idempotency guard.
antipattern.fix: Add idempotency key to payload. Persist 'processed_at' against the key in a DB table; reject retries that match a processed key.

antipattern.symptom: Worker pool exhausted; tasks back up for hours because one task hung on an HTTP call.
antipattern.root-cause: Defaults assumed; soft_time_limit + time_limit never set.
antipattern.fix: Set soft_time_limit = p95 latency * 2 and time_limit = soft + 30s. Verify with a fault-injected hung-call test.

antipattern.symptom: Low-priority report jobs starved time-sensitive email tasks.
antipattern.root-cause: All tasks went to default queue; single worker pool.
antipattern.fix: Declare explicit queues per task. Spin up workers with `-Q emails -Q reports` and per-queue concurrency settings.
